1: YHWH answers the prayer of Isaiah 63:15-64:12 with a reversal: I let myself be sought by those who did not ask for me; I let myself be found by those who did not seek me. The divine passive — YHWH making himself available — precedes any human search. The declaration to those who did not ask is the pattern of pure grace: YHWH's initiative is prior to and not conditioned by human seeking. Paul quotes this verse in Romans 10:20 as the pattern of Gentile reception of the gospel — those who were not seeking YHWH found him.
2: All day long I spread out my hands to a rebellious people — the divine posture of invitation persistently rejected. The spread-out hands are both welcome (come to me) and lamentation (look at what you are doing). The description of the rebellious: walking in ways not good, following their own schemes — not ignorance but willful self-direction away from YHWH.
3: A people who continually provoke me to my face — the challenge is not covert but overt, in YHWH's direct sight. The specific provocations: sacrificing in gardens and burning incense on brick altars. These are the syncretistic practices of those who blend YHWH-worship with Canaanite and Babylonian fertility-cult. The provocation is intensified by its being in YHWH's face — shameless, not hidden.
4: Who sit among the graves — necromantic practice, seeking guidance from the dead (cf. Isaiah 8:19). Who eat pork and contaminated broth — explicit violations of Levitical food laws (Leviticus 11:7-8). The practices combine the religiously transgressive (necromancy) with the ritually impure (pork). Together they profile a community that has wholesale abandoned covenant distinctiveness.
5: Who say, Stay away — do not come near me, for I am holier than you! The irony: those who commit the most explicit impurities claim superior holiness. The self-designation as holy (qodesh) when they are the farthest from it is the deepest form of religious self-deception. These are smoke in my nostrils, a fire that burns all day — the sensory image of YHWH's sustained displeasure.
6: See, it stands written before me: I will not be silent — I will repay; I will pay it fully into their laps. The written-before-me is covenant-legal language: the charges are recorded. YHWH's refusal to be silent (contrast 62:1 where YHWH refuses silence for Zion's sake — here he refuses silence in judgment) grounds the judgment as deliberate and unavoidable.
7: Your iniquities and your fathers' iniquities together — the LORD (YHWH, disp 4) declares — because they burned incense on the mountains and reviled me on the hills. The generational guilt: the children inherit the consequences of the fathers' covenant violations. The mountains-and-hills worship is the recurring marker of the Canaanite high places that Israel adopted.
8: As new wine is found in a cluster and someone says, Do not destroy it — a blessing is in it — so I will do for the sake of my servants, and not destroy them all. The remnant principle: even in the cluster of bad grapes there is good wine worth preserving. YHWH's judgment is not totalistic; his servants are the living wine hidden in the apostate cluster. The servant/servants distinction: the whole community is addressed, but within it a remnant of faithful servants is preserved.
9: I will bring forth from Jacob a people, and from Judah an inheritor of my mountains; my chosen ones shall possess it, and my servants shall dwell there. The chosen-and-servants vocabulary links back to chapters 41-53: the servant who suffered (53) now becomes the community of servants who inherit. The mountains are both the hills of Judah and the eschatological mountain of YHWH's house (Isaiah 2:2-3).
10: Sharon shall become a meadow for flocks, and the Valley of Achor a resting place for cattle — for my people who have sought me. The two geographical markers span the width of the promised land: Sharon on the Mediterranean coast, Achor (meaning trouble) in the Jordan Valley. The valley of trouble (Joshua 7:24-26, Hosea 2:15) becomes a door of hope — a place of rest rather than cursing. The land itself is restored for those who sought YHWH.
11: But you who forsake YHWH (YHWH, disp 4) and forget my holy (qodesh, disp 2) mountain, who set a table for Fortune and fill cups of mixed wine for Destiny — the apostate community practices divination-feasting dedicated to Babylonian fortune-gods (Gad and Meni). The specific identification of the gods marks this as Babylonian syncretism from the exile. Forsaking YHWH and forgetting his mountain are the active forms of the passive wandering of v 2.
12: I will destine you to the sword, and you will all bow down for slaughter, because when I called you did not answer; when I spoke you did not listen. The judgment is proportional to the rejection: YHWH called (spread his hands, vv 1-2); they did not answer. The refusing-to-answer reverses the covenant dialogue of Isaiah 55:6-7 — YHWH was near and available; they did not seek him.
13: Therefore the Lord GOD declares: My servants shall eat, but you will go hungry; my servants shall drink, but you will be thirsty. The servant-contrast: everything the servants receive (eating, drinking, rejoicing, singing, good names) is the inverse of what the apostates experience (hunger, thirst, shame, crying, wailing, death). The chapter divides humanity into exactly two categories.
14: My servants shall shout for joy of heart, but you shall cry out in pain of heart and wail in anguish of spirit (ruach, disp 4). The ruach-anguish of the apostate is the inverted image of the Spirit-joy of the servant (Isaiah 61:1-3). The servant community is characterized by Spirit-life and Spirit-joy; the apostate community is characterized by ruach-anguish — spirit broken, not filled.
15: You will leave your name behind as a curse for my chosen ones; and the Lord GOD will put you to death, while his servants will be called by a different name. The name-given (to YHWH's servants) and the name-left-as-a-curse (by the apostates) are parallel to the new-name promise of 62:2 and Isaiah 65:16. The chosen ones and servants receive a new, blessing-bearing name; the apostates become the vocabulary of cursing.
16: Whoever blesses himself in the land will bless himself by the God of faithfulness (emet, truth/faithfulness, disp 2 at Isaiah 61:8), and whoever takes an oath will swear by the God of faithfulness (elohim, disp 4). The God-of-faithfulness title in vv 15-16 (twice) is unique in the OT. Emet (faithfulness, truth) is what YHWH promised to give at Isaiah 61:8 — his covenant-dealing is emet. Because the former troubles are forgotten, the God of faithfulness can be the ground of all blessing-speech and oath-taking.
17: For see, I am creating new heavens and a new earth; the former things will not be remembered or come to mind. The new creation declaration: the same bara (create) used in Genesis 1:1 and Isaiah 40:28 — YHWH's creative act, not renovation. The forgetting of former things is the eschatological amnesia that makes the new creation a true new beginning, not a continuation. Peter (2 Peter 3:13) and John (Revelation 21:1) pick up this promise directly.
18: Be glad and rejoice forever in what I am creating; for see, I am creating Jerusalem to be a joy, and her people a delight. The forever-rejoicing is olam-gladness — the same permanent joy of Isaiah 35:10, Isaiah 51:11, Isaiah 61:7. The new creation's center is the new Jerusalem: the city is not merely included in the new creation but is its focal point and the site of its greatest joy.
19: I will rejoice in Jerusalem and be glad in my people; no more shall the sound of weeping or the voice of distress be heard in her. The divine rejoicing mirrors the community's rejoicing — YHWH's delight in Zion (Isaiah 62:4-5) is reciprocated by Zion's delight in YHWH. The elimination of weeping and distress is the comprehensive definition of shalom: every form of unraveling is healed.
20: Never again will there be an infant who lives only a few days, or an old man who does not complete his years; for the young man who dies at a hundred will be considered a youth, and the one who does not reach a hundred will be considered accursed. The eschatological longevity: infant mortality (the most vivid form of life-cut-short) and premature adult death are both abolished. A hundred years is the measure of an incomplete life — the new creation extends life expectation beyond what any present calculation allows.
21: They shall build houses and live in them; they shall plant vineyards and eat their fruit. The covenant-blessing in its most concrete form: the work of one's hands benefits oneself. This reverses the covenant curse of Deuteronomy 28:30 (you will build a house but another will live in it; you will plant a vineyard but not harvest it). The new creation restores the full benefit of human labor within covenant.
22: They will not build only for others to inhabit; they will not plant only for others to eat — the Deut 28 curse is completely reversed. For like the days of a tree shall the days of my people be, and my chosen ones shall long enjoy the work of their hands. The tree-days life-span: ancient trees (cedar, oak) live for centuries. The chosen ones' lives extend to tree-scale duration — the image of deep-rooted permanence and slow, patient growth into full fruiting.
23: They shall not toil for nothing or give birth to children who meet disaster; for they are the offspring of those whom YHWH (YHWH, disp 4) has blessed, and their descendants with them. The generational blessing: the children of the covenant community inherit blessing, not curse. The Deut 28 curses included futile labor and afflicted children; both are reversed. The offspring-of-the-blessed is the Abrahamic vocabulary (Genesis 12:2-3) applied to the restored community.
24: Before they call, I will answer; while they are still speaking, I will listen. The eschatological acceleration of prayer: YHWH answers before the word is formed. This is the maximum expression of divine attentiveness — the inverse of the lament that YHWH was not responding (Isaiah 63:15-19, Isaiah 64:12). The silence of YHWH under which the community prayed is replaced by an anticipating presence that meets the prayer before it is voiced.
25: The wolf and the lamb will graze together, and the lion will eat straw like the ox; dust will be the serpent's food. They will neither harm nor destroy in all my holy (qodesh, disp 2) mountain, says YHWH (YHWH, disp 4). The restoration of Edenic peace echoes Isaiah 11:6-9 — the same predator-prey reconciliation marks the final state as a return-to-garden condition. The holy mountain is the place of covenant presence; in it all violence is eliminated. The serpent-eating-dust (Genesis 3:14) is not undone but incorporated into the peaceable order — the serpent remains in its cursed state while all others are restored. Rev 21-22 inhabits this vision as the new-Jerusalem image of the final state.
